title: 12x16 Greenhouse with Twinwall Glazing
summary: A stick-framed 12x16 greenhouse glazed in twinwall polycarbonate — real stud walls, real trusses, a storm door. House framing at garden scale.
description:
This is not a kit and not a hoop house — it's a stick-framed building, 12 by 16 feet, framed exactly like a shed and then glazed with twinwall polycarbonate instead of sided. If you can frame a wall (or are ready to learn on something forgiving), nothing here is exotic: bottom plates, studs on layout, a ridge with rafters at 30°, and plywood only up to knee height where it holds the walls square.

The polycarbonate is the material decision that shapes everything else. Twinwall diffuses light, holds heat dramatically better than film or glass, and comes in 4x8 sheets that span the 1x4 purlins — but it has manufacturer rules that override any plan: which face out (UV coating), sealing the fluted ends, fastener type and torque. Read your product's instructions before the framing is even done, because purlin spacing and blocking exist to serve the glazing. Budget honestly too: the polycarbonate and its channels cost more than all the lumber combined, and it's the reason this build sits in the top cost tier.

Two site realities before the first cut. Any bottom plate that touches concrete or ground must be treated lumber — untreated plates rot from the slab up and you won't see it happen. And a 12x16 outbuilding is permit territory in many places; one phone call to the local building office beats disassembling a greenhouse.
timeLabel: 40–80 hrs — several weekends, two people for walls and roof

tools.note: All the framing cuts, plus ripping plywood into the 24" knee-wall strips.
The 30° and 60° truss and rafter cuts want a saw with a reliable angle stop.
16D and 8D framing nails — or skip it and use structural screws throughout, which are easier solo.
The roof trusses go together with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws.
A 4-footer. Walls get stood, plumbed, and braced — the level is the difference between a building and a lean.
Marking rafter angles and keeping stud layout honest.

materials.name: 2x4, 16 ft
materials.note: Side wall plates — do not cut, the full length is the point. Treated where in contact with ground or concrete.

materials.name: 2x4, 12 ft
materials.note: Front and back bottom plates. Treated if on ground/concrete.

materials.name: 2x6, 16 ft
materials.note: The ridge. Full length, no splice.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Studs and blocking. Expect to buy a few more for blocking once the glazing layout is real.

materials.name: 1x4, 16 ft
materials.note: Roof purlins — the polycarbonate screws to these. 8-footers work but add joints.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/2" exterior, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Knee-wall sheathing, ripped into 24" strips — it holds the walls square.

materials.name: Twinwall polycarbonate, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: The single biggest line item, and worth pricing before committing — sheet prices vary a lot by thickness and supplier.

materials.name: H-channel, 8 ft
materials.note: Joins polycarbonate sheets edge to edge.

materials.name: Polycarbonate end-sealing strips
materials.note: Per the sheet manufacturer — open flutes grow algae and condensation.

materials.name: Rain gutters with downspouts
materials.note: Catch the roof runoff — a rain barrel here waters the greenhouse.

materials.name: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws
Polycarbonate fasteners
materials.note: Per the glazing manufacturer — the source build used roofing screws with sealing washers.

cutList.note: Top and bottom, both side walls. Treated for bottom plates on ground/concrete.
Rip more strips as needed for the front and back walls.
Both ends 30° off square, parallel, long point to short point. Bottom foot cut comes later, in the steps.
Both ends 60° off square, NOT parallel, long point to long point.
Long-point measurement, top cut to a 30° point — see the plan diagram before cutting.
Both ends 30° off square, parallel, long point to short point; birdsmouth foot cut in the steps.

steps.title: Frame the two side walls
steps.body:
Build both 16-foot side walls flat on the ground: full-length top and bottom plates, studs and 4x4 end posts on layout, fastened with 3" nails or screws.

If the greenhouse sits on concrete or bare ground, the bottom plates must be treated lumber — or add a sacrificial treated plate below. Untreated pine against a slab wicks water and rots invisibly.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Square the walls with the knee sheathing, then stand them
steps.body:
Rack each wall until its diagonals match, then nail the 24" plywood strips along the base with 1-3/4" screws or 8D nails — the plywood is what LOCKS the square you just created.

Stand the walls and brace them with 2x4s at an angle to stakes or the slab. They stay braced until all four walls are tied together.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Frame the front wall around the door
steps.body: Build the front wall leaving a 36" rough opening for the storm door — or whatever YOUR door's instructions call for. Check the actual door's rough-opening spec before framing; storm doors are unforgiving about openings.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Frame the back wall
steps.body: Same as the front, minus the door opening. If you want powered ventilation later — and a 12x16 greenhouse in summer will make you want it — this is the wall to plan a vent or fan opening into now.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Stand and tie all four walls
steps.body:
Stand the front and back walls, level and plumb everything with the 4-foot level, and fasten the corners together. Add plywood at the corners and temporary diagonal bracing until the roof goes on.

Get plumb right here. Every truss and rafter cut assumes these walls are vertical; a half-inch lean becomes a gap at the ridge.

steps.title: Build the two gable trusses
steps.body: Assemble the two end trusses — 30° top chords, 60° bottom chord ends, king post — with 1-1/2" pocket holes and the exterior 2-1/2" pocket screws. Build the second directly on top of the first so they match exactly.

steps.title: Set the trusses, eaves, and ridge
steps.body:
Screw the trusses to the tops of the end walls from underneath, angling screws into the side wall tops. Run the eave 2x4s along the side wall tops and fasten the trusses to their ends.

Mark the ridge board's rafter layout on the ground — it's a one-minute job flat and a ladder acrobatics job in the air — then set the ridge between the gable trusses. A temporary block under each ridge end (or a metal hanger) holds it while you fasten.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Cut the rafter feet
steps.body: The rafters are cut to length with parallel 30° ends per the cut list; now cut the birdsmouth foot on each so it seats on the eave. Cut one, test it in place, and use it as the pattern for the other thirteen — never measure fourteen rafters individually.

steps.title: Hang the rafters
steps.body: Fasten each rafter at the eave and the ridge, and screw up from under the side walls into the rafter feet. At the ridge some rafters will land opposite each other — toenail those at an angle. Work in opposing pairs so the ridge stays straight instead of being pushed sideways rafter by rafter.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Square the building and add blocking
steps.body:
Check the whole structure for square and rack it true with temporary bracing where needed. Then add 2x4 blocking at the front, back, and sides wherever the twinwall's edges and fasteners will need backing.

This is the step where the glazing manufacturer's instructions take over from the plan — read them now, not on the roof.

steps.title: Run the purlins
steps.body: Screw the 1x4 purlins across the rafters, working from the bottom of the roof up, two 1-3/4" screws per crossing. These are what the roof glazing screws into, so their spacing must match your sheet manufacturer's span rating.
materials: Framing nails, 16D and 8D, or exterior structural screws

steps.title: Glaze the walls
steps.body:
Framing done — now the twinwall. Your sheet's instructions win every argument with this plan: UV-coated face out, ends sealed with the proper strips, manufacturer-specified fasteners, snug but never dimpling the sheet.

On the front and back, start at the center and work outward so any shortage lands at the corners where trim covers it. Cut the door opening and gable angles with a utility knife or fine blade. Join sheets edge-to-edge with H-channel — and know going in that getting sheets INTO the channel is a fight; the source build's fix was rubbing the channel edges with a bar of soap, and it works. Sides run vertical per manufacturer's direction.

steps.title: Glaze the roof
steps.body: Measure the actual as-built roof length, including the overhang the gutters need, before cutting roof sheets. Seal the top edges per the manufacturer, then screw the sheets to the purlins with sealing-washer fasteners. Do not overdrive — a dimpled washer is a future leak.

steps.title: Trim the corners and cap the ridge
steps.body: Work from the bottom up: metal corner trim on the vertical corners and eaves, ridge cap over the peak. The trim isn't cosmetic — it covers every cut sheet edge and closes the corner gaps you deliberately left in the glazing step.

steps.title: Hang the door and finish out
steps.body:
Install the storm door per its instructions, then the gutters and downspouts — 32 feet of roof sheds a lot of water, and directing it into a rain barrel waters the greenhouse for free.

The source build later added cedar siding over the plywood knee walls, and a back-wall vent for airflow. Both are worth it; neither is urgent. Grow things.

images.alt: Stick-framed 12x16 greenhouse with translucent twinwall polycarbonate walls and roof
